Vision

vi 
 
Preface 
The Physics Teaching Module for Forms 2 is designed to guide the teachers on how to deliver 
Competence-based lessons to facilitate the understanding of Physics concepts, fostering a deep 
appreciation for the role of Physics in everyday life and its applications in various fields. This module 
aims to develop a solid foundation in Physics and cultivate critical thinking, analytical skills, and 
problem-solving strategies. Subsequently, apply Physics concepts to real-world problems and 
emerging technologies through the engagement of learners in hands-on, hearts-on and minds-on 
practical activities and simulations to reinforce theoretical understanding. 
 
This Physics Teaching Module for Form 2 intends to create a stimulating and supportive learning 
environment where teachers can prepare lessons with a profound understanding of Physics. The 
module will guide the teachers to support learners grow intellectually and personally by preparing 
them for professions in science and technology as well as for higher education by inculcating 
curiosity, critical thinking, and practical skills. 
 
It is hoped that this module will inspire teachers to prepare lessons which are competence based with 
the fascinating world of Physics and appreciate its significance in shaping the future. 
 
 
 
Kelvin Mambwe, (PhD) 
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services  
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

vii 
 
Acknowledgement 
This Physics Teaching Module is designed to provide the teacher with a scope and sequence of topics 
considered necessary to be offered at Secondary School level in Form 2 of term one (1) and term two 
(2). This is with a view of providing guidance to the teaching and learning of this unique, but yet 
existing blend of concepts of Physics for teachers and other experts in the field to appropriately offer 
relevant lessons at Secondary ordinary level. 
 
Many thanks go to individuals, institutions and Organisations that participated in the successful 
development of this module. These include; teachers, lecturers from colleges, public universities in 
Zambia and in particular, the University of Zambia. Sincere gratitude also goes to the Directorate of 
Secondary Education and National Science Centre in the Ministry of Education for their support and 
collaboration during the consultation period. 
 
Last but not the least, the commitment and hard work of all the staff at the Curriculum Development 
Centre in ensuring that this module comes to reality is recognised. 
 
 
 
Charles Ndakala, (Dr.) 
Director – Curriculum Development 
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

x 
 
How to use this Module 
To effectively use this Physics Teaching Module for Form 2: 
 Read and familiarise yourself with the module's content, learning activities, and assessment 
guides. 
 Plan your lessons in advance, using the module's suggested teaching and learning 
activities. 
 Use a variety of teaching methods, including demonstrations, discussions, group work, and 
hands-on experiments. 
 Encourage active learning by asking open-ended questions, promoting critical thinking, 
and fostering problem-solving skills. 
 Assess learners learning regularly, using the module's suggested assessment strategies and 
tools. 
 Provide feedback and support to learners, helping them to identify areas for improvement 
and develop their skills. 
 Integrate technology into your teaching, using multimedia resources and interactive 
simulations to enhance student engagement and understanding. 
 Monitor student progress and adjust your teaching strategies as needed, to ensure that all 
students meet the learning objectives 
By following these steps, you can effectively use this Physics Teaching Module for Form 2 to support 
your teaching and promote learning

xi 
 
Introduction 
The Physics Teaching Module for Form 2 provides guidance to teachers on how to deliver 
competence-based lessons on key Physics concepts in line with the syllabus. The module focuses on 
Mechanics II and Thermal Physics, which help learners understand motion, forces, energy, heat, 
and temperature, and how these concepts explain many natural and technological processes. It 
supports teachers in preparing lessons that help learners understand the natural, artificial, and 
technological environment while developing interest in Physics.  
 
The module promotes learner-centred teaching approaches that encourage inquiry, curiosity, 
experimentation, and problem-solving. Through carefully designed learning activities, teachers are 
supported in helping learners develop critical thinking, analytical reasoning, innovation, and practical 
scientific skills. These competences enable learners to apply scientific principles to everyday 
situations and technological advancements. 
 
The study of Physics Concepts in Mechanics are widely applied in engineering, transport, 
construction, and mining industries, while Thermal Physics plays an important role in energy 
production, manufacturing, food processing, and environmental management. In Zambia, these 
applications support key sectors such as mining, energy, infrastructure development, agriculture, 
and industrial production, which are essential for economic growth and sustainable development. 
Through this module, learners are expected to develop a strong understanding of Physics principles 
and acquire the knowledge and skills necessary for further studies and careers in science, technology, 
and related fields. 
 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials 
The study of Physics content requires hands-on experiences, visual aids, and interactive resources to 
strengthen deep understanding and appreciation. To support the teaching and learning of Physics in 
Form 1, the suggested teaching and learning materials are meant to: 
 Enhance learner engagement and motivation in Physics 
 Develop practical skills and laboratory techniques 
 Promote critical thinking, problem-solving, and analytical skills 
 Support differentiated instruction and inclusive learning 
 
The suggested teaching and learning materials are either artificial or natural. By utilising these 
suggested teaching and learning materials, teachers can create a dynamic and supportive learning 
environment that promotes academic excellence, creativity, and scientific literacy in Physics for Form 
1 learners.

xii 
 
Learning Environment Set Up 
To create an effective learning environment for teaching and learning Physics it is important to 
deepen understanding of the concepts and application in real life context. The suggested learning 
environment set-up aims to create a safe, inclusive, and engaging space.  
 Natural Environment: A natural learning environment is a setting where learners 
explore and learn naturally, often without explicit instruction or formal teaching, such as 
in school surroundings 
 Man-Made Environment: Man-made learning environments are intentionally 
designed safe spaces, such as classrooms, laboratories, and libraries, designed for 
formal instruction, hands-on activities, and games and songs 
 Technological Learning Environment: Access educational apps, and software for 
learning, including game-based platforms, virtual platforms, and simulations, to engage 
learners and promote learning 
 
Safety in the Learning Environment 
Safety in the learning environment is a requirement in learning Physics, as learners are exposed to 
potential hazards during hands-on experiments. Collaboration between teachers, and learners, is 
important to create a responsible learning environment that promotes scientific inquiry. Guidelines 
for maintaining a safe learning environment include laboratory safety rules (protocols), risk 
identification, personal protective equipment, emergency response plans, storage, and disposal, 
and learner responsibilities. Prioritising safety minimises risks, prevents accidents, and ensures a 
positive learning experience. 
 
Teaching Methodology 
The effective teaching methodologies in STEM Physics include: 
 Conducting experiments: demonstrate key principles and encourage curiosity among 
learners. 
 Collaborative learning: Pair learners to work together, promoting peer-to-peer teaching, 
discussion, and problem-solving. 
 Conceptual learning: Connect Physical concepts to everyday life, industry, or current events, 
making learning relevant and meaningful. 
 Differentiated instructions: Tailor teaching to meet diverse learning styles, abilities, and 
interests of different learners. 
 Feedback and Reflection: Encourage learners to reflect on their learning, providing 
constructive feedback to guide improvement. 
 Inquiry-based learning: encourage learners to explore, investigate, and discover Physics 
concepts through hands-on experiments and activities.  
 Integration of Technology: Use digital tools, simulations, and visualisations to enhance 
engagement, understanding, and analysis.

xiii 
 
 Problem-based learning: Present real-world problems or case studies, requiring learners to 
apply Physics principles to develop solutions. 
 Project-based learning: Assign open-ended projects, allowing learners to design, conduct, 
and present research or applications of Physics concepts. 
 
By implementing these methodologies, a teacher can create an engaging, inclusive, and effective 
STEM Physics learning environment. 
 
Learning Activities 
Learning activities are intentional educational experiences aimed at promoting learning, engagement, 
and achievement among learners. Facilitated by teachers, they help acquire new knowledge, skills, 
attitudes, and behaviour change. To create an inclusive environment, teachers should use a "hook" or 
problem posing or scenario or key question or case studies to introduce new learning activities in an 
interactive and interesting way. 
 
Time Allocation 
The standard minimum teacher - learner contact time for Physics at Secondary School Level is four 
(4) hours per week, translating into Six (6) periods. The duration for a single period is forty (40) 
minutes. The contact time at Secondary School Level is planned in such a way as to give ample time 
for practical activities. 
 
Icons used in this Module  
This module utilises icons as visual symbols or graphics to represent instructions, enhancing the 
learning experience for learners. Icons categorise and organise instructions, making navigation easier 
for teachers.

xiv 
 
Assessment  
This assessment shall include a variety of methods to evaluate the competences of learners in terms of 
knowledge, skills, and general understanding of scientific concepts. The assessment approach will 
integrate both formative and summative components to support ongoing competence development and 
final achievement measurement. Summative assessment will be used to evaluate learners’ learning at 
the end of the O level Physics course to measure their achievements against specific competences 
through Final Examinations. In order to help teachers and learners identify areas where learners need 
more support or revision, formative assessment will be used to track learner progress and knowledge 
throughout the teaching and learning process. 
 
However, assessments shall follow the following pattern: 
 School Based Assessment (SBA) shall comprise of assignments, projects, practical work, 
research and end of term tests during the period of study and as guided by the Examinations 
Council of Zambia (ECZ). This shall carry 30% of the total marks.  
 Summative assessment shall carry 70% of the total marks. 
The Examinations Council of Zambia (ECZ) shall prepare detailed procedures or guidelines on how 
SBA will be conducted by the teachers and the management of the assessment results. The 
standardised national examination shall be administered at the end of Form 4 by the Examination 
Council of Zambia. 
 
Key Competences  
In Physics the following key competences are the fundamental abilities and qualities that will enable 
individual learners to: 
 Manage their own learning and knowledge. 
 Interact with others and solve problems. 
 Contribute to society and the economic development of the country. 
 Adapt to change and navigate through emerging issues in the environment.

xv 
 
 
KEY COMPETENCE 
DESCRIPTOR 
Analytical Thinking  
To analyse and interpret data, making evidence-based conclusions. 
Collaboration  
To work together, promoting peer-to-peer teaching, discussion, and 
problem-solving. 
Communication  
To communicate scientific information effectively, both orally and in 
writing. 
Creativity and 
Innovation  
To create new ideas and products by applying processes and introducing 
new techniques that can add value. 
Critical Thinking  
To enhance learners’ ability to think critically and solve problems 
through logical reasoning based on conclusions. 
Digital literacy  
Using a broad range of Information and Communication Technologies 
such as a cell phone, computer and calculator in specific contexts. 
Environmental 
Sustainability 
To apply physical principles to understand and mitigate the 
environmental impact of human activities. 
Problem Solving 
To use scientific knowledge, critical thinking, and analytical skills to 
develop a robust problem-solving mindset, enabling learners to tackle 
complex challenges and drive innovation in various fields.

TOPIC 2.1: MECHANICS 2 
INTRODUCTION 
Mechanics is the branch of Physics that deals with the motion of objects and the forces acting upon 
them. It is all about understanding how objects move, respond to forces and interact with their 
environment. It is divided into kinematics (describing motion) and dynamics (explaining why objects 
move). Mechanics 2 comprises of four (4) sub-topics; Work, Energy and Power, Linear Momentum, 
Simple Machines and Pressure. 
 
Key Competence(s):  
 Analytical Thinking 
 Collaborative 
 Communication  
 Critical Thinking  
 Problem Solving 
 Real world application 
 Environmental sustainability 
 Creativity and innovation 
 
SUB-TOPIC 2.1.1: WORK, ENERGY AND POWER 
INTRODUCTION 
 
Work, energy and power are fundamental concepts that describe the relationships between forces, 
motion and energy transfer. The study of Work, Energy and Power helps learners understand how 
machines operate, how energy is transferred and how different tasks are performed efficiently in 
everyday life. This sub-topic has nine learning activities as sequenced in the 2024 Form 1-4 Physics 
syllabus, page 10. 
Specific Competence  
 Create a system that applies work, energy, and power concepts. 
Key terms 
  Work is the product of the force applied and the distance moved in the direction of the force.

3 
 
 A joule is the amount of work done when a force of 1N moves an object through a distance of 
1m in the direction of the force. 
 Energy is the ability or capacity to do work.  
 Mechanical energy is the sum total of an object’s kinetic energy (KE) and potential energy 
(PE). 
 Potential energy is the energy possessed by a body due to its position or condition. 
 Gravitational Potential energy is the work done in raising an object against the earth’s 
gravitational attraction. 
 Elastic Potential Energy Is the energy possessed by all elastic materials such as stretched 
spring, wire or rubber band. 
 Kinetic energy is the energy possessed by a body due to its velocity or motion. 
 Power is the rate of doing work. 
 A watt is the rate of transfer of energy of one joule per second. 
Learning activities 
This sub-topic has nine learning activities from the syllabus focusing on learning through hands-on 
activities and problem-solving tasks.  
Activity 2.1.1.1: Developing a system that maximises the mechanical energy (a simple 
pendulum, inclined plane, and water tank…) 
This activity is designed to help learners develop a system that maximises the mechanical energy. In 
this activity, learners will design and build a system that maximises the mechanical energy. 
 
 
 
 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials 
Natural Materials: Wood (branches, logs), rope, stones or rocks, water, ... 
Artificial Materials: Metal wires or nails, synthetic rope/metallic chain, plastic buckets or containers, old 
tires or rubber bands, ... 
Learning environment set up 
 Natural Environment such as school surroundings.  
 Artificial Environment such as the laboratory or any room or place where learners can be such 
as mobile laboratory, classroom, ....  
HOOK: Imagine you are told to design a simple swing using local materials that 
converts potential energy to kinetic energy efficiently and safely.  How would 
you develop this system?”

4 
 
Teacher’s role(s) 
 Introduce the concept of mechanical energy (e.g. in a simple pendulum, inclined plane, and 
water tank…) through a scenario 
 Put learners in small groups 
 Provide the materials to the learners in groups 
 Guide learners to identify local resources 
 Guide learners in designing and testing their systems 
 Provide feedback on energy conversion 
 Ensure safety  
Learners’ Tasks 
 Researching and Brainstorming on the given scenario of mechanical energy (e.g in a simple pendulum, 
inclined plane, water tank, ...) 
 Identifying local materials 
 Designing and testing the system 
 Getting feedback on energy conversion 
 Ensuring safety protocols 
 Presenting their findings 
Assessment Guide  
 Evaluate system design 
 Assess understanding of energy conversion 
 Observe demonstration of problem-solving and critical thinking skills 
Skills Developed  
 Problem-solving: Designing systems for maximising mechanical energy 
 Critical thinking: Analysing energy conversions 
 Collaboration: Working in groups as they were designing and identifying materials to use 
 Communication: Presenting their findings 
 Application: Real-world energy optimisation 
Key Learning Points 
 Mechanical Energy = KE + PE 
 Systems can maximise energy through:

5 
 
i. 
Pendulum: Optimal length (best possible length for maximum efficiency), amplitude 
(best possible amplitude for maximum efficiency) 
ii. 
Inclined plane: Optimal angle (best possible angle for maximum efficiency) 
iii. 
Water tank: Optimal height (best possible height for maximum efficiency) 
 Energy conversions (KE to PE or PE to KE) affect efficiency 
Activity 2.1.1.2: Determining the work done on an object by a force. 
This activity is designed to help learners determine the work done on an object by a force by carryout 
an experiment. This will help explain many everyday situations. 
 
 
 
 
 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials 
Natural Materials: Wood (for ramps, levers, or pulleys), rocks or stones (as weights), rope (from 
plants), ... 
Artificial Materials: Metal (weights or hooks, pulleys, levers, ...), spring scales (for measuring force), 
measuring tapes or rulers, ... 
Learning environment set up 
 Natural Environment such as school surroundings, forest, ...  
 Artificial Environment such as the laboratory or any room or place where learners can be such 
as mobile laboratory, classroom, ... 
Teacher’s Role(s) 
 Introduce an activity through a hook  
 Put learners in small groups 
 Provide materials to the learners in groups 
 Guide learners to design experiments to determine the work done on an object by force 
 Guide learners in measuring force and distance 
 Facilitate the determination of work done  
 
 
HOOK:  A learner designed a simple pulley system to lift a 50 kg bag of maize to a 
height of 2 meters using a rope and a wooden beam. How would you determine the 
work done on a bag?

6 
 
Learners’ Tasks 
 Designing experiments to determine the work done on an object by force apply forces  
 Measuring force (using spring scales) and distance (using a meter rule) 
 Determining work done using the formula, Work done = Force x distance. 
 Comparing results and discuss real-world applications 
Assessment Guide  
 Review calculations of work done (W= F × d; W = F × d × cos θ; F × d × sin θ). Explain the 
conditions how these formulae apply. 
 Observe demonstration of problem-solving in groups 
Skills Developed  
 Analytical thinking: Calculating work done with correct units 
 Problem-solving: Applying W = F × d × cos θ 
 Critical thinking: Considering force and direction 
 Application: Real-world examples (e.g., lifting, pushing) 
Key Learning Points 
 Work is done when force moves an object in the direction of action of force 
 Work done (W) = F × d 
 Units: Joules (J) = Nm 
 θ is the angle between force and displacement 
 Work is scalar quantity, can be positive (+), negative (-), or zero (0) 
 Examples: lifting weights over a distance, pushing carts over a distance, ... 
Activity 2.1.1.3: Investigating the relationship between force and displacement through 
experimenting, W = Fs. 
This activity is designed to help learners investigate the relationship between force and displacement 
through experiment. 
 
 
 
HOOK: “A rubber band and a ruler were used to investigate the relationship 
between stretch (displacement) and applied force. What pattern do you observe?

7 
 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials 
Natural Materials: Rulers (wooden) or sticks, stones, ... 
Artificial Materials: Spring scales (for measuring force), rulers, rubber bands, measuring tapes, ... 
Learning environment set up 
 Natural Environment such as school surroundings, forest, ...  
 Artificial Environment such as the laboratory or any room or place where learners can be such 
as mobile laboratory, classroom, ....  
Teacher’s role(s) 
 Introduce an activity through a hook  
 Put learners in small groups 
 Provide the materials to the learners in groups 
 Guide learners to investigate the relationship between force and stretch during an experiment. 
 Encourage data collection through measurements 
 Encourage data tabulation and checking the pattern 
 Encourage plotting the graph and checking the trends  
 Facilitate discussions on the relationship between force and displacement 
 Ask learners to present their findings 
 
Learners’ Tasks 
 Investigating the relationship between force and stretch using an experiment 
 Collecting data through measurements 
 Tabulating and checking the data patterns 
 Plotting the graph and checking the trends  
 Discussing the relationship between force and displacement 
 Present their findings 
 
Assessment Guide  
 Observe group experiments (setup, data collection, tabulation) 
 Review calculations of work done (W = F × s) 
 Evaluate understanding of force-displacement graphs

8 
 
Skills Developed  
 Scientific inquiry: Investigating the relationship between applied force and displacement 
through an experiment 
 Analytical thinking: Evaluating data patterns and trends 
 Critical thinking: Setting up an experiment to investigate the relationship between force and 
displacement 
 Collaboration: Working in groups to investigate the relationship between force and 
displacement 
 Communication: Presentation of findings 
Key Learning Points 
 W = F × s (when F is constant and parallel to s) 
 Work = Area under Fs graph 
 Units: Joules (J) = Nm 
 Understanding applied force in relation to stretch 
Activity 2.1.1.4: Conducting experiments to measure and calculate mechanical energy (kinetic 
and potential) in different systems, Ep = mgh and Ek =  mv2. 
This activity is designed to help learners conduct experiments to measure and calculate mechanical 
energy (kinetic and potential) in different systems, Ep = mgh and Ek =  mv2. 
 
 
 
 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials 
Natural Materials: Stone, ramps (wooden planks, inclined paths), ... 
Artificial Materials: Stopwatches, ramps, measuring tapes, ... 
HOOK: Picture this: A bunch of boys are kicking a football ⚽ on top of a steep hill 
🏔. One of them nails a powerful kick, and the ball rolls down the slope like a wild 
ride 🎢. 
Discuss what happens to its 'energy of movement' and 'energy of position?

9 
 
Learning environment set up 
 Natural Environment such as school surroundings, ... 
 Artificial Environment such as the laboratory or any room or place where learners can be such 
as mobile laboratory, classroom, ....  
Teacher’s role(s) 
 Introduce an activity through a hook  
 Put learners in small groups 
 Provide the materials to the learners in groups 
 Guide learners on how to conduct an experiment to measure and calculate mechanical energy. 
 Guide on measurement and data collection 
 Facilitate the calculations of mechanical energy 
 Facilitate discussions on energy conversions 
 Ask learners to present their findings 
Learners’ Tasks 
 Conducting an experiment to measure and calculate mechanical energy 
 Measuring and collecting data 
 Calculating mechanical energy 
 Discussing on energy conversions 
 Presenting their findings 
Assessment Guide  
 Observe experiment setup and data collection 
 Review calculations of KE =  mv², PE = mgh 
 Evaluate understanding of energy conversions 
 Use of appropriate units (Joules) 
Skills Developed  
 Scientific inquiry: Conducting an experiment 
 Analytical thinking: Calculating KE, PE, energy values from collected data 
 Data analysis: Interpreting energy conversions (mechanical energy)

10 
 
Key Learning Points 
 KE =  mv² where m=mass and v= velocity 
 PE = mgh where m= mass, g= gravity and h= height 
 Energy conversions: KE to PE or PE to KE. 
 Systems of mechanical energy: Pendulums, ramps, falling objects 
 Energy conversion application: Elevated water tank system 
Activity 2.1.1.5: Demonstrating the law of conservation of energy in mechanical energy. 
This activity is designed to help learners demonstrate the law of conservation of energy in mechanical 
energy 
 
 
 
 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials 
Natural Materials: Stones or fruits, string or rope, tree branches (for pendulums), ... 
Artificial Materials: Stopwatches, string or rope, measuring tapes, ... 
Learning environment set up 
 Natural Environment such as school surroundings, ... 
 Artificial Environment such as the laboratory or any room or place where learners can be such 
as mobile laboratory, classroom, ....  
Teacher’s role(s) 
 Introduce an activity through a hook  
 Put learners in small groups 
 Provide the materials to the learners in groups 
 Guide learners to design pendulum experiments 
 Encourage observation and discussion 
 Guide learners on measurement of mass of the bob, height and time 
 Facilitate calculations of PE maximum and KE maximum 
HOOK: During a science lesson, a teacher takes learners outside and ties a small 
stone to a strong string. The teacher begins to swing the stone in a vertical circle and 
asks the learners to carefully observe the motion and think about what is happening 
to the energy of the stone during its movement

11 
 
 Instruct learners to compare calculated PE maximum and KE maximum 
 Ask learners to present their findings 
Learners’ Tasks 
 Designing a simple pendulum experiment 
 Measuring of mass of the bob, height and time 
 Calculating PE maximum and KE maximum from measured data 
 Comparing calculated PE maximum and KE maximum 
 Presenting their findings 
Assessment Guide  
 Observe experiment setup (e.g., pendulum, …) 
 Review calculations of KE maximum and PE maximum 
 Evaluate understanding of energy conservations 
 Use of appropriate units (Joules) 
Skills Developed  
 Scientific inquiry: Designing experiments 
 Analytical thinking: Calculating KE maximum and PE maximum 
 Data analysis: Comparing calculated PE maximum and KE maximum 
 Critical thinking: Explaining energy conservations 
Key Learning Points 
 Law of Conservation of Energy: Total energy is constant 
 KE + PE = Constant (ideal systems) 
 Energy conversions: KE to PE or PE to KE (e.g., pendulum) 
 Real systems of energy conversations: Water tank supply system, … 
Activity 2.1.1.6: Exploring how machines can change the amount of mechanical energy 
required to perform a task, such as cranes. 
This activity is designed to help learners explore how machines can change the amount of mechanical 
energy required to perform a task, such as cranes.

12 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials 
Natural Materials: Sticks, rocks, strings or ropes, bamboo, ... 
Artificial Materials: Cardboard, pulleys, levers, weights, crane, rope, ... 
Learning environment set up 
 Natural Environment such as school surroundings, ... 
 Artificial Environment such as the laboratory or any room or place where learners can be such 
as mobile laboratory, classroom, ....  
Teacher’s role(s) 
 Introduce an activity through a hook  
 Put learners in small groups 
 Ask the learners to look for sticks, rocks and bamboo and later provide other materials 
(Cardboard, pulleys, levers, weights, rope) to the learners 
 Facilitate groups to explore how machines can change the amount of energy required to 
perform a task e.g. a crane lifting loads 
 Ask guiding questions (e.g., "How does using a pulley affect effort?") 
 Facilitate sharing of findings 
 Ensure safety, encourage adjustments 
Learners’ Tasks 
 Looking for sticks, rocks and bamboo 
 Collecting other materials (Cardboard, pulleys, levers, weights, rope) 
 Exploring how machines can change the amount of energy required to perform a task e.g. a 
crane lifting loads 
 Responding to guiding questions (e.g., "How does using a pulley affect effort?") 
 Sharing of findings through a presentation or a discussion. 
 Following safety protocols  
HOOK: During harvest time, imagine carrying a 90Kg bag of maize, lifting and loading 
the bag onto a truck by hand. Imagine also the same task being done using a machine 
such as a crane, pulley, or lifting device.  
a. How does it make work easier in farms and also at storage facilities?  
b. Where else does this situation apply?

13 
 
Assessment Guide  
 Observe group work and ask guiding questions 
 Assess ability to explore and explain machine's effect on effort 
  Evaluate presentations on machine effectiveness 
Skills Developed  
 Inquiring: Exploring how machines can change the amount of mechanical energy 
 Critical thinking: Analysing effort vs. load 
 Collaboration: Working in groups 
 Practical skills: Using machines such as a crane 
 Communication: Presenting their findings 
Key Learning Points 
 Machines can change the amount of mechanical energy needed for tasks to be performed 
 Pulleys and levers reduce effort for heavy loads 
 Mechanical advantage of a machine helps perform tasks easily in daily life (like construction 
cranes) 
Activity 2.1.1.7: Calculating the efficiency of energy conversion using the appropriate 
formula, efficiency = 
   x 100%. 
This activity is designed to help learners calculate the efficiency of energy conversion using the 
appropriate formula, efficiency = 
   x 100%.  
 
 
 
 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials  
Natural Materials: Sunlight 
HOOK: You light a bulb that consumes X electrical energy to produce Y heat and Z 
light energy. 
 How would you determine the efficient of the bulb?

14 
 
Artificial Materials: Batteries, bulbs, resistor, connecting wires, solar panels (optional), ammeter, 
voltmeter, switch, ...

conservation of energy to show that total energy is conserved (even if the system is not 100% 
efficient).

Analyse how this system applies work, energy, and power concepts to improve farming efficiency. 
Question 2  
A community in Eastern Province is building a hand-operated water pump to draw water from a 15 
m deep borehole for irrigation and domestic use. 
a) Create a simple lever-pump system (e.g., using a long handle as a class 1 lever) and calculate the

b) Explain energy conservation as one side goes down (GPE lost = GPE gained on other side +

a) For the hand-crank: design a gear system to increase rotational speed. If operator applies 80 N

SAMPLE QUESTIONS ANSWERS 
Question 1  
a) A single movable pulley (or block-and-tackle with mechanical advantage 2) halves the effort. 
Effort force = weight / 2 = (50 × 10) / 2 = 250 N (distance rope pulled = 8 m for 4 m lift).   
b) Energy changes: Chemical energy (from farmer’s muscles), kinetic energy (of bag during lift), 
gravitational potential energy (GPE = mgh = 50 × 10 × 4 = 2000 J) at top. Some energy lost as 
heat/sound due to friction/muscle inefficiency. Conservation: Input energy (work done by farmer) 
= GPE gained + losses.   
c) Work done = 2000 J (against gravity). Power = work/time = 2000 / 8 = 250 W. The system 
applies work (force × displacement), converts chemical → mechanical → potential energy, and 
calculates power to show rate of energy transfer, helps farmer work faster, reduce fatigue, store 
more maize safely.

21 
 
Question 2  
a) Work done against gravity = mgh = 10 × 10 × 15 = 1500 J per stroke.   
b) Efficiency = useful output / input × 100%. Input work = 1500 / 0.60 = 2500 J. Lost energy = 
1000 J (mainly friction in pump seals/pipes + heat in muscles).   
c) Longer handle increases mechanical advantage, lower effort force but more distance moved, 
same work. Power = work/time; slower pumping lowers power demand (less fatigue). System 
applies work (to overcome gravity/friction), energy conversion (muscular, potential), power to 
match human capability, improves water access, reduces drudgery. 
Question 3   
a) Pedalling → kinetic energy of bike/rider → rotational → dynamo → electrical energy. KE = 
½mv² = ½ × 85 × 25 = 1062.5 J.   
b) Time (ideal) = energy / power = 3600 / 10 = 360 s (6 min). With 40% efficiency, input power 
needed ≈ 25 W, actual pedalling time longer (~15 min).   
c) Applies work (force on pedals × distance), energy conversion (KE → electrical), power (rate of 
charging). Provides sustainable charging, supports education (night study), small businesses 
(phone money), reduces kerosene use. 
Question 4   
a) Moments balance: 50 × 10 × 1.5 = 50 × 10 × d, d = 1.5 m (symmetrical). Work by gravity on 
descending student = mgh = 50 × 10 × 0.8 = 400 J.   
b) GPE lost by A = 400 J, converted to GPE gain by B + KE of both + friction/heat losses. Total 
mechanical energy conserved if no losses.   
c) Power ≈ work/time = 400 / 2 = 200 W (average over transfer). Demonstrates work (force × 
distance), energy forms/conversion/conservation, power (rate), great for teaching concepts 
interactively. 
Question 5 
a) Work per revolution = force × distance = 80 × (2π × 0.3) ≈ 80 × 1.885 = 150.8 J.   
b) Useful energy = mgh = 5 × 10 × 0.2 = 100 J (plus some kinetic). Efficiency = 100 / 1500 × 
100% ≈ 6.7% (low due to friction; gears help).   
c) Applies work (torque × angle), energy conversion (muscular → rotational → mechanical), 
power (sustained rate). Backup ensures reliability; gears optimise power input vs output, reduces 
manual labour, increases shelling speed, boosts cooperative income.

SUB-TOPIC 2.1.2: LINEAR MOMENTUM 
INTRODUCTION 
Linear momentum is the product of an object's mass and velocity. It's a vector quantity, meaning it 
has both magnitude and direction. It is calculated using the formula:  
ρ = mv where ρ = linear momentum, m=mass (kg) and v= velocity (m/s). 
Specific competence  
 Apply the principle of linear momentum in everyday life. 
Key terms 
 Momentum is a measure of an object's tendency to keep moving in a straight line. 
 Linear Momentum (ρ): The product of an object's mass (m) and velocity (v). (ρ = mv) 
 Conservation of Momentum: Total momentum remains constant in a closed system (no 
external forces). 
 Impulse (J): Change in momentum, equals force × time. (J = ∆ρ or J = F x ∆t) 
 Elastic Collision: Momentum and kinetic energy conserved; objects bounce apart. 
 Inelastic Collision: Momentum conserved; kinetic energy not conserved; objects may stick 
together. 
 Vector Quantity: Momentum has magnitude and direction. 
 Units: kgm/s (kilogram meters per second). 
Learning activities 
This sub-topic has five learning activities from the syllabus focusing on learning through hands-on 
activities and problem-solving tasks.

SAMPLE QUESTIONS 
Question 1  
During a road traffic accident investigation in Kabwe, police analyse a collision between a 1200 kg

c) Analyse how understanding linear momentum helps improve road safety features in vehicles used

Question 2  
A fisherman in Lake Kariba uses a dugout canoe. When he throws a 2 kg fish horizontally backwards 
at 5 m/s relative to the canoe, the canoe (mass 80 kg including fisherman) moves forward. 
a) Apply conservation of linear momentum to explain why the canoe moves forward and calculate its 
velocity (assume initial momentum zero, ignore water resistance).   
b) If the fisherman throws the fish in 0.4 s, calculate the average force on the fish and on the 
fisherman/canoe system.

or pushing off in ice skating. 
Question 3

a) Use conservation of momentum to calculate the velocity of the tackled player after the collision 
(assume elastic for simplicity, but discuss realism).   
b) Calculate the change in momentum for each player and explain the impulse involved.   
c) Analyse why understanding momentum helps coaches and players reduce injury risk in contact 
sports like football or rugby in Zambian schools. 
Question 4   
A rocket-propelled fireworks seller in Kasama launches small rockets. The rocket (mass 0.5 kg empty)

a) Apply conservation of momentum to find rocket velocity just after exhaust ejection (assume starts

b) Explain why rockets work in space (no air) but fireworks need initial launch.   
c) Analyse how this principle is applied in modern transportation (e.g., jet engines in aircraft flying 
to/from Zambian airports). 
Question 5  
In a Zambian mine transport system near Solwezi, a 2000 kg loaded truck moving at 8 m/s collides 
with a stationary 1500 kg empty truck. They couple together after impact. 
a) Calculate the common velocity after collision using conservation of momentum.   
b) Discuss whether momentum is conserved if external forces (friction) act during collision.   
c) Analyse the importance of understanding linear momentum in designing safer mining haulage roads 
and vehicle braking systems in Zambia. 
SAMPLE QUESTIONS ANSWERS 
Question 1  
a) Momentum before = momentum after (isolated system, no external forces in horizontal direction).   
Momentum before = (1200 × 15) + (800 × 0) = 18 000 kg m/s   
Momentum after = (1200 + 800) × v = 2000 v   
So, 18 000 = 2000 v, v = 9 m/s (matches given value).   
b) Inelastic because kinetic energy is not conserved (vehicles stick together). KE before = ½ × 1200 × 
225 = 135 000 J (car has 0). KE after = ½ × 2000 × 81 = 81 000 J. Lost KE = 54 000 J (converted to 
heat, sound, deformation).

33 
 
c) Principle explains why head-on collisions are dangerous (high momentum transfer). Safety features 
like crumple zones increase collision time, reduce force (impulse = change in momentum = F × Δt), 
seatbelts/airbags spread force over body, reducing injury. In Zambia, enforcing these in public 
transport reduces fatalities. 
Question 2  
a) Total initial momentum = 0.   
Momentum after: fish + canoe = 0   
(2 × –5) + (80 × v) = 0, –10 + 80v = 0, v = 0.125 m/s forward. Recoil due to conservation, action-
reaction (Newton's third law).   
b) Impulse on fish = change in momentum = 2 × 5 = 10 Ns (from rest to 5 m/s). Average force = 
impulse / time = 10 / 0.4 = 25 N backward on fish. Equal/opposite force (25 N forward) on 
fisherman/canoe.   
c) Principle: when you push backward on boat/ice, you gain forward momentum. Explains why 
jumping off a small boat pushes it away, common in fishing or crossing rivers in rural Zambia. 
Question 3  
a) Momentum before = 60 × 4 + 55 × 0 = 240 kg m/s   
Momentum after = 60 × (–1) + 55 × v = –60 + 55v   
240 = –60 + 55v, 55v = 300, v ≈ 5.45 m/s forward. (Real tackle inelastic, some energy lost.)   
b) Δp tackler = 60 × (–1 – 4) = –300 kg m/s (loss). Δp tackled = 55 × 5.45 ≈ +300 kg m/s (gain). 
Impulse = Δp (force × time from collision).   
c) High momentum change, high force if short time, injury (e.g., concussions). Tackling technique 
(low body, longer contact) increases Δt, reduces F. Helmets/pads help. Coaches teach safe techniques 
to apply principle. 
Question 4   
a) Initial momentum = 0.   
After: rocket (0.5 × v) + gases (0.2 × –40) = 0   
0.5v – 8 = 0, v = 16 m/s forward. Recoil propels rocket.   
b) Conservation holds in vacuum, exhaust momentum backward = rocket forward. Fireworks use 
gunpowder explosion for initial impulse + air resistance/gravity.

34 
 
c) Jet engines eject high-speed gases backward, plane forward momentum. Explains efficient long-
distance flights (e.g., to Johannesburg). Principle key to aviation safety/efficiency in Zambia. 
Question 5  
a) Momentum before = 2000 × 8 + 1500 × 0 = 16 000 kg m/s   
After = (2000 + 1500) × v = 3500 v   
v = 16 000 / 3500 ≈ 4.57 m/s.   
b) Approximately conserved if collision time short (external impulse small compared to collision 
impulse). Friction reduces total slightly but often ignored for approximation.   
c) Explains rear-end collision risks on mine roads. Brakes increase stopping time, reduce force on 
occupants. Speed limits, distance keeping, and vehicle design (crumple zones) apply principle to 
prevent injuries/fatalities in heavy transport.

35 
 
SUB-TOPIC 2.1.3: SIMPLE MACHINES 
  INTRODUCTION 
Simple machines are mechanical devices that makes work easier. These include tools such as, screw 
driver, scissors, wheelbarrow, bottle opener, wrench, hoe, cooking sticks, stapler, tweezers, seesaw ... 
In this sub-topic, learners will study different types of simple machines and how they operate. 
Specific Competence:  Build simple machines to solve real life problems. 
Key terms: 
 Load (L): The weight of an object being moved by a simple machine. 
 Effort (E): Is the force applied in moving the load. 
 Mechanical advantage (MA): Is the ratio of the load to effort, MA = L/E 
 Velocity ratio (VR): Is the ratio of distance moved by effort (dE) to the distance moved 
by load (dL), VR = dE/dL 
 Efficiency (ղ): Is the ratio mechanical advantage to the velocity ratio expressed as a 
percentage, ղ = MA/VR x 100% 
Learning Activities 
 The four learning activities in this sub-topic introduces learners to explore various simple machines by 
designing and building them. Various calculations will be done using the designed simple machines to 
calculate Mechanical advantage, Velocity ratio and efficiency.

SAMPLE QUESTIONS 
1. A farmer in Kitwe wants to lift a 400 kg sack of fertilizer onto a truck. He uses a first-class lever made 
from a long pole with the fulcrum placed 1 m from the sack (load arm = 1 m) and the effort arm = 4 
m. The weight of the load is 4000 N (g = 10 m/s²).   
(a) Calculate the Mechanical Advantage (MA).   
(b) If the farmer moves the effort end 4 m down, how far does the sack rise? Calculate the Velocity 
Ratio (VR).   
(c) Assuming the machine is 80 % efficient, calculate the actual effort required.

43 
 
2. Workers at a construction site in Copperbelt Province use a block-and-tackle pulley system with 4 
ropes supporting the load to lift a 1200 N steel beam to the second floor. The effort applied is 400 N.   
(a) Calculate the Mechanical Advantage.   
(b) The effort moves 8 m while the beam rises 2 m. Calculate the Velocity Ratio.   
(c) Calculate the efficiency of the system and explain why it is less than 100 %.   
3. A mechanic uses an inclined plane (ramp) to push a 2000 N car up a 5 m long ramp to a height of 1 m 
instead of lifting it vertically.   
(a) Calculate the Velocity Ratio of the ramp.   
(b) If the actual effort needed is 600 N, calculate the Mechanical Advantage.   
(c) Calculate the efficiency and state one real-life advantage of using the ramp.   
4. screw jack is used to lift the front of a 10 000 N truck for tyre repair. The screw has a pitch of 5 mm 
and the effort arm (handle) is 0.4 m long. One complete turn of the handle raises the truck 5 mm.   
(a) Calculate the Velocity Ratio.   
(b) If the actual effort applied is 200 N, calculate the Mechanical Advantage.   
(c) Calculate the efficiency of the screw jack.   
5. A bicycle uses a gear system (wheel and axle combined with gears). The rear wheel radius is 0.35 m 
and the pedal sprocket radius is 0.08 m. When the cyclist applies force to the pedals, the rear wheel 
turns once for every 4.375 turns of the pedals.   
(a) Calculate the Velocity Ratio of the gear system.   
(b) If the load (resistance) at the wheel is 300 N and the effort at the pedal is 120 N, calculate MA.   
(c) Calculate efficiency and explain how gears help cyclists on hills.   
 
SAMPLE QUESTIONS ANSWERS 
Answer 1   
(a) MA = Load / Effort (ideal case)   
Since effort arm / load arm = 4 m / 1 m, ideal MA = 4.   
(b) VR = distance moved by effort / distance moved by load   
VR = 4 m / 1 m = 4.

44 
 
(c) Actual effort = Load / (MA × efficiency)   
Efficiency η = MA / VR × 100 % (but here we use given η = 80 %)   
Actual effort = 4000 N / (4 × 0.8) = 4000 / 3.2 = 1250 N.   
This lever solves the real-life problem of lifting heavy loads with less force – exactly as required by 
the syllabus learning activity. 
Answer 2   
(a) MA = Load / Effort = 1200 N / 400 N = 3.   
(b) VR = distance moved by effort / distance moved by load = 8 m / 2 m =4   
(c) η = (MA / VR) × 100 % = (3 / 4) × 100 % = 75 %   
The efficiency is less than 100 % because of friction in the pulleys and weight of the movable pulleys 
themselves – common in real-life pulley systems built to solve lifting problems. 
Answer 3  
(a) VR = length of ramp / height = 5 m / 1 m = 5  
(b) MA = Load / Effort = 2000 N / 600 N ≈ 3.33.   
(c) η = (MA / VR) × 100 % = (3.33 / 5) × 100 % ≈ 66.6 %.   
Advantage: The ramp reduces the force required (from 2000 N to 600 N), making it easier and safer 
for one person to move the car – a classic application of the inclined plane. 
Answer 4 
(a) Distance moved by effort in one turn = circumference of handle = 2π × 0.4 m ≈ 2.513 m.   
VR = distance moved by effort / distance moved by load = 2.513 m / 0.005 m = 502.6   
 
(b) MA = Load / Effort = 10 000 N / 200 N = 50   
(c) η = (MA / VR) × 100 % = (50 / 502.6) × 100 % ≈ 9.95 %   
(The low efficiency is due to high friction in the screw thread – typical for screw jacks used in 
garages.)

45 
 
Answer 5   
(a) VR = radius of effort wheel / radius of load wheel (or turns ratio) = 0.35 / 0.08 = 4.375              
(matches the given ratio).   
(b) MA = Load / Effort = 300 N / 120 N = 2.5   
(c) η = (2.5 / 4.375) × 100 % ≈ 57.1 %   
Gears increase VR on hills so the cyclist can apply smaller force over a longer distance – exactly how 
gears solve real-life transportation problems.

SUB-TOPIC 2.1.4: PRESSURE 
INTRODUCTION  
Pressure is an important concept in Physics. It explains how forces act over surfaces and within fluids 
(liquids and gases). Acquiring knowledge on pressure helps learners to explain everyday phenomena 
such as why sharp knives easily cut through materials as opposed to blunt one; why dams are made 
thicker at the bottom, how juice or water move up through drinking straws into our mouths, and how 
hydraulic press machines operate. However, this module introduces pressure in solids and fluids and 
its application in our everyday-life. 
 
Specific competence 2.1.4.1  
 Apply the principles of pressure to solve real-life problems 
 
Key terms  
 Pressure: A force acting normally (at 90o angle) per unit area. 
 Force: A push on an object. 
 Area: Two-dimensional space. 
 Pascal (Pa): A force of 1N acting normally per unit area (1m2). In short, it is 
2

 Density (ρ): Mass (m) per unit volume (V), ρ = V
m 
 Upthrust: Upward force due to fluid 
 Flotation:  Is when a body is suspended in a fluid (neither sinking down the fluid nor leaving 
the fluid body) 
 Law of Flotation:  A floating body displaces a fluid of weight equal to its own weight 
 Pascal’s principle: Pressure applied at one point in an enclosed fluid at rest is transmitted 
equally to all parts of the fluid.    
 Archimedes’ principle: When a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences 
an upthrust which is equal to the weight of the displaced fluid.

EXPECTED STANDARD 
SAMPLE QUESTIONS 
1. A miner carries a 120 kg load on his head using a flat board (area 0.15 m²). Calculate the 
pressure on his head. (g = 9.8 m/s²)  
2. During rainy season, a farmer notices that a tractor with narrow tires sinks deeper into muddy 
soil than one with wide tires, even though both carry the same weight. Explain this using the 
concept of pressure.  
3. A woman wearing high-heeled shoes (heel area 1 cm² = 0.0001 m²) and weighing 600 N stands 
on soft ground. Calculate the pressure under each heel and explain why she might sink more 
than if wearing flat shoes (heel area 100 cm²).  
4. In a Zambian market, a heavy sack of mealie meal (weight 500 N) is placed on a wooden pallet 
with base area 0.8 m². Calculate the pressure on the floor. If the sack is tipped onto a smaller 
area of 0.2 m², what happens to the pressure and why might the floor crack?  
5. A sharp knife cuts through meat easily, but a blunt one does not, even with the same force. 
Explain this difference in terms of pressure, and suggest how a chef in Lusaka could improve 
cutting efficiency without increasing force  
SAMPLE QUESTIONS ANSWERS 
   Answer 1:  
   Force = weight = mass × g = 120 kg × 9.8 m/s² = 1176 N   
   Pressure = F / A = 1176 N / 0.15 m² = 7840 Pa (or 7.84 kPa)   
   (Working must be shown for full marks.)

64 
 
   Answer 2:   
   Pressure = Force / Area. Both tractors have the same weight (force), but narrow tires have smaller 
contact area → higher pressure → greater force per unit area on the soft mud → tractor sinks deeper. 
Wide tires spread the weight over larger area → lower pressure → less sinking. 
   Answer 3: 
   Assuming weight on two heels: Force per heel = 600 N / 2 = 300 N   
   Pressure (high heel) = 300 N / 0.0001 m² = 3,000,000 Pa (3 MPa)   
   Pressure (flat shoe) = 300 N / 0.1 m² = 3,000 Pa 
   The high heel produces ~1000 times higher pressure, much greater tendency to sink into soft ground. 
   Answer 4:   
   Original pressure = 500 N / 0.8 m² = 625 Pa  
   New pressure = 500 N / 0.2 m² = 2500 Pa (4 times higher)   
   Pressure increases because the same force is concentrated over a smaller area, higher risk of cracking 
weak wooden floors. 
   Answer 5  
   Pressure = Force / Area. Sharp knife has very small contact area at the edge, very high pressure → 
cuts easily. Blunt knife has larger contact area → lower pressure →does not cut well.   
   Suggestion: Sharpen the knife (reduce area) or use a serrated knife to concentrate force.

TOPIC 2.2: THERMAL PHYSICS 
INTRODUCTION 
Thermal Physics is the branch of Physics that studies the thermal properties of matter, including 
temperature, heat, and the behaviour of systems in relation to thermal energy. This topic introduces 
learners to important concepts that explain how heat affects matter and how thermal energy is 
transferred and utilised in everyday life. Knowledge of Thermal Physics is important in many 
practical applications such as cooking, food preservation, engine operation, energy production, and 
industrial processes. Thermal Physics covers six sub-topics: Simple Kinetic Theory of Matter, 
Measurement of Temperature, Expansion of Solids, Liquids and Gases, the Internal Combustion 
Engine, Heat Transfer, and Measurement of Heat. These sub-topics help learners develop an 
understanding of how thermal energy influences the behaviour of matter and how this knowledge can 
be applied in science, technology, and everyday life. 
Key Competence(s):  
 Analytical Thinking 
 Collaborative 
 Communication  
 Critical Thinking  
 Problem Solving 
 Real world application 
 Environmental sustainability 
 Creativity and innovation 
 
SUB-TOPIC 2.2.1 SIMPLE KINETIC THEORY OF MATTER 
INTRODUCTION 
Matter is made up of tiny particles (atoms or molecules) that are in constant random motion, with 
spaces between them. The particle’s motion and interactions explain the properties of solids, liquids 
and gases. 
Specific competence  
 Analyse the impact of simple kinetic theory of matter on technological innovations 
Key terms 
 Kinetic theory of matter: Is a scientific model that explains the behavior of matter by stating 
that particles (atoms or molecules) are in constant motion, and their energy relates to 
temperature and phase changes.

67 
 
 Gas leak detector: Is a device that senses the presence of gas (e.g., methane, propane) in the 
air to alert users of a potential leak, preventing explosions or poisoning. 
 Thermal insulators: These are materials that reduce the transfer of heat between objects or 
environments, keeping heat in or out to improve energy efficiency (examples include foam, 
fiberglass, or aerogel). 
 Refrigeration system: Is a mechanical setup that removes heat from a space or substance to 
lower its temperature, typically using a refrigerant that cycles through compression, 
condensation, evaporation, and expansion. 
 Molecular model of matter: Is a representation that describes substances as composed of 
molecules with specific arrangements and interactions, used to predict physical and chemical 
properties. 
 Brownian motion: Is the random, erratic movement of microscopic particles suspended in a 
fluid (liquid or gas), caused by collisions with fast‑moving molecules in the surrounding 
medium. 
 Diffusion: Is the process by which particles spread from an area of high concentration to an 
area of low concentration, driven by random molecular motion until equilibrium is reached. 
 Evaporation: Is the phase transition where liquid molecules gain enough energy to escape 
the surface and become vapor, typically cooling the remaining liquid. 
 Cooling effect of evaporation: Is the temperature drop that occurs when a liquid evaporates, 
because the highest‑energy molecules leave the surface, reducing the average energy 
(temperature) of the remaining liquid. 
Learning activities 
This module has four learning activities from the syllabus which have been expanded into nine 
activities, focusing on competence-based learning through hands-on activities and problem-solving 
tasks.

SAMPLE QUESTIONS 
Question 1   
A Zambian company is manufacturing affordable double-walled stainless steel vacuum flasks 
(thermos) for households and street vendors to keep tea or nshima warm for many hours without 
reheating. 
a) Using the simple kinetic theory of matter, explain why heat loss is greatly reduced in a vacuum 
flask compared to an ordinary single-walled container.   
b) Analyse how this technological innovation improves daily life and the economy in areas with 
limited electricity or firewood.   
c) Suggest one limitation of this technology. 
Question 2  
Engineers in Lusaka are designing a more efficient compressed natural gas (CNG) system for public 
minibuses to replace diesel use.

83 
 
a) Explain, using the kinetic theory of matter, why compressing natural gas into high-pressure 
cylinders allows a large volume of fuel to be stored in a small space.   
b) Analyse the impact of this technology on Zambia’s transport sector and environment.   
c) Describe what happens to the temperature of the gas during rapid filling according to kinetic 
theory, and why cooling is sometimes needed. 
Question 3  
A food processing plant in Zambia is installing industrial spray dryers to turn fruit pulp (mango, 
banana) into powder for longer shelf life and export. 
a) Using kinetic theory, explain how the rapid evaporation in the spray dryer lowers the temperature 
of the tiny liquid droplets.   
b) Analyse how this innovation helps Zambian farmers and the export economy.   
c) Explain why the drying air must be hot according to the kinetic theory. 
Clue: Need to take an educational tour to a processing plant to have experiential link to the 
application of kinetic theory of matter. 
Question 4  
A factory is producing aerosol mosquito repellents using compressed gas propellants for household 
and agricultural use. 
a) Using the simple kinetic theory of matter, explain how pressing the nozzle releases the liquid as a 
fine mist.   
b) Analyse the positive and negative impacts of this technology on public health in Zambia.   
c) Suggest how kinetic theory helps manufacturers make the product safer. 
Question 5  
Solar-powered water pasteurisation units are being distributed in rural Zambian communities to kill 
bacteria in drinking water without electricity or fuel. 
a) Explain, using kinetic theory, why heating water to approximately 65 to 70 °C for a few minutes is 
sufficient to make it safe, even though it is not boiling.   
b) Analyse the broader impact of this technology on community health and education.   
c) Describe what happens to particle motion when the water cools after pasteurisation.

84 
 
ANSWERS TO SAMPLE QUESTIONS 
Question 1  
a) According to the simple kinetic theory, heat transfer by conduction occurs when fast-moving 
particles in a hot substance collide with slower particles and transfer kinetic energy. Convection 
occurs when heated gas/liquid particles rise, carrying kinetic energy. In the vacuum flask, the space 
between the double walls has no (or very few) gas particles, almost no particles to collide and 
transfer energy, conduction and convection are virtually eliminated. Only slow radiation remains 
(often reduced by shiny surfaces).   
b) The innovation allows people to carry hot food/drinks safely for long periods, reduces need to re-
boil water or re-heat food, saves charcoal, electricity, or paraffin. It lowers household costs and 
deforestation pressure. Street vendors keep food hot longer, sell more, increase income. In clinics, it 
helps keep water hot for hygiene.   
c) Limitation: Radiation still occurs slowly (cannot be completely stopped), so contents eventually 
cool. Very high initial cost for quality flasks may limit access in poorest households. 
Question 2 
a) Kinetic theory states gas pressure is caused by particles colliding frequently and forcefully with 
container walls. Compressing the gas forces particles closer together, more frequent and harder 
collisions per unit area, much higher pressure. At high pressure, the same number of particles 
occupies far less volume, large amount of gas stored in small cylinders.   
b) Impact: Lower fuel cost per kilometer, cheaper public transport fares, benefits low-income 
passengers. Cleaner burning than diesel, reduces particulate matter and CO₂ in urban areas like 
Lusaka, improves air quality and public health. Supports local gas industry growth.   
c) Rapid compression does work on the gas, increases average kinetic energy of particles, 
temperature rises significantly (can become dangerously hot). Cooling systems (fans or heat 
exchangers) are used to remove excess heat and prevent damage to cylinders or valves. 
Question 3 
a) In liquids, particles have a range of kinetic energies. The fastest-moving particles at the surface 
escape (evaporate), average kinetic energy of remaining particles decreases, temperature of droplets 
drops (evaporative cooling).   
b) Powder has very long shelf life (no water for microbes), farmers sell surplus fruit instead of losing 
it to spoilage, higher income. Export of dried fruit powder brings foreign currency, supports national 
economy and job creation in processing plants.   
c) Hot air provides high kinetic energy to gas particles, increases collision rate and energy transfer to 
liquid droplets, speeds up evaporation rate so drying happens quickly before droplets spoil or stick. 
Question 4

85 
 
a) Inside the sealed can, gas particles are under high pressure (frequent, forceful wall collisions). 
When the nozzle opens, gas particles rush out rapidly, dragging liquid particles with them → liquid 
breaks into tiny droplets (aerosol mist).   
b) Positive: Widespread use reduces malaria transmission, fewer hospital cases, healthier workforce 
and school attendance. Negative: Inhaling propellants or overuse can cause respiratory irritation; 
improper disposal of cans risks explosion or environmental harm.   
c) Manufacturers control pressure (by adjusting gas/liquid ratio) so it is high enough for good spray 
but not dangerously high, reduces explosion risk. Use safer propellants with lower flammability. 
Question 5 
a) Bacteria and pathogens are proteins/enzymes. Heating increases kinetic energy of water particles, 
more frequent and energetic collisions with pathogen molecules, denatures (unfolds) proteins, 
kills/inactivates them. 65 to 70 °C for minutes achieves this without needing 100 °C boiling (saves 
energy/time).   
b) Safer drinking water, sharp drop in diarrhea, cholera, typhoid, fewer child deaths and school 
absences, better education and productivity. Reduces firewood/charcoal use, less deforestation and 
smoke-related lung disease.   
c) As water cools, average kinetic energy of particles decreases, particles move slower, fewer 
collisions, lower pressure and slower diffusion. Dissolved gases may come out of solution if cooled 
rapidly.

86 
 
SUB-TOPIC 2.2.2: MEASUREMENT OF TEMPERATURE 
INTRODUCTION 
Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles of a substance (the degree of 
hotness or coldness of a body). The S.I unit of temperature is a kelvin (K). Temperature is measured 
using a thermometer. 
Specific competence  
 Measure temperature using appropriate instruments 
Key terms 
Lower fixed point: This is the temperature of pure melting ice at standard atmospheric pressure. 
Upper fixed point: This is the temperature at which pure water boils under normal atmospheric 
pressure 
Ice point: Is the temperature of pure melting ice at one atmosphere. 
Steam point:  Is the temperature of pure boiling water at one atmosphere. 
Thermometer: Is an instrument used to measure temperature. 
Boiling point: Is the temperature at which saturated vapour pressure of a liquid is equal to the 
external pressure. 
Melting point: Is the temperature at which a substance changes from solid to liquid. 
Calibration: Is the process of standardising unmarked thermometer to give accurate temperature 
readings. 
Temperature scale: Is a system for measuring temperature in Celsius, Kelvin scales, .... 
Physical properties: These are characteristics such as volume, density, electrical resistance, and gas 
pressure that change with temperature. 
Impurities effect: Is the influence of impurities on the boiling and melting points of substances, 
typically raising or lowering them. 
Pressure effect: Is the influence of external pressure on the boiling and melting points of substances. 
Thermometric Liquids: These are liquids used in Liquid-in-Glass Thermometers.

SAMPLE QUESTIONS ANSWERS 
Question 1   
a) The most appropriate is the clinical thermometer (mercury-in-glass type with a constriction/kink). 
It is designed for the narrow range 35 °C to 42 °C, has a constriction to prevent mercury from falling 
back after removal, and gives accurate readings to 0.1 °C.   
b) Procedure: Shake the thermometer to bring mercury below 35 °C; place under the tongue (or 
armpit); leave for 3 to 5 minutes; remove and read the scale at eye level; disinfect after use.   
c) Advantage: Cheap, no batteries needed, reliable in power-outage areas. Limitation: Contains toxic 
mercury (risk if broken), slow reading (3 to 5 min), requires close contact (infection risk in 
outbreaks), infrared is faster and non-contact. 
 
 
Question 2   
a) It has two bulbs: alcohol (expands/contracts) connected to mercury thread. As temperature rises, 
alcohol pushes mercury up (records max on one scale); as it falls, alcohol contracts and mercury is

104 
 
pulled back (records min on the other scale). Metal indices are pushed to extremes and stay at 
maximum and minimum positions.   
b) Reset by using a magnet or tilting to move indices back to current mercury levels.   
c) It records extremes automatically over time (useful for daily/weekly monitoring without constant 
checking); simple thermometer only gives instant reading. Helps farmer avoid frost damage or heat 
stress to crops. 
Question 3   
a) Laboratory thermometer (alcohol-in-glass or mercury-in-glass) with range -10 °C to 110 °C (or 
higher).   
b) Steps: Secure thermometer in cork with bulb above water but below steams level; heat water 
steadily; stir; read temperature when steady (boiling); read meniscus at eye level; avoid touching hot 
bulb.   
c) Alcohol preferred: lower freezing point (measures below 0 °C if needed), non-toxic (safer if 
broken), cheaper; mercury is toxic and has higher freezing point but better for high precision in some 
ranges. 
Question 4   
a) It detects infrared radiation emitted by the skin (all objects emit IR proportional to temperature); 
converts radiation intensity to temperature reading using calibration.   
b) Hold 3 to 5 cm from forehead (or point to temporal artery); ensure no hair or sweat obstruction; 
press trigger; read display instantly.   
c) Advantages: Fast (seconds), non-contact (hygiene, reduces infection risk), no consumables. 
Limitation: Affected by external factors (sweat, air conditioning, direct sun) and less accurate than 
clinical; needs batteries or charging. 
Question 5  
a) Thermocouples measure very high temperatures (up to 1000s °C), respond quickly, are rugged, 
and can be used in harsh environments (steam, vibration).   
b) Two dissimilar metals joined at a junction, temperature difference between measuring junction (in 
oven) and reference junction produces small voltage; voltage is measured and converted to 
temperature.   
c) Impact: Precise continuous monitoring, consistent baking, better quality biscuits, less waste. 
Workers avoid opening hot ovens repeatedly, safer; liquid-in-glass cannot handle high temperatures, 
would break, and is not remote or readable.

SUB-TOPIC 2.2.3: EXPANSION OF SOLIDS, LIQUIDS AND GASES 
 
INTRODUCTION 
Thermal expansion is the increase in size (length, area, or volume) of a substance when its 
temperature rises. When matter is heated, its particles gain kinetic energy, move faster, and spread 
farther apart. This causes the substance to expand. When cooled, the particles lose kinetic energy, 
move closer together, and the substance contracts. However, when water is heated at 0o C it contracts 
until the temperature reaches 4oC, beyond that point water expands like any other substances. 
Specific Competence:  Demonstrate expansion of solids, liquids and gases. 
 
  Key terms 
 Anomalous Expansion: This is the abnormal expansion experienced by water only. 
 Kinetic energy: Is the energy that an object or particles have because of motion.  
 Boyle’s law states that for a fixed mass of gas at constant temperature, the pressure (P) of the gas 
is inversely proportional to its volume (V), 
  
 Charles’s law states that for a fixed mass of gas at constant pressure, the volume (V) of the gas is 
directly proportional to its absolute temperature (T), 
 
 Gay-Lussac’s law states that for a fixed mass of gas at constant volume (V), the pressure of the 
gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature (T), 
 
 The Ideal Gas Law states that for a fixed mass of gas the product of pressure (P) and volume (V) 
is direct proportional to the absolute temperature (T), expressed as

SAMPLE QUESTIONS 
1. In a school laboratory, a teacher uses a ball-and-ring apparatus made of brass. When both are at room 
temperature, the ball passes through the ring. The ball is then heated with a Bunsen burner for 2 
minutes. 
a) Describe step-by-step how this apparatus demonstrates thermal expansion of solids.   
b) If the same ball is made of steel (linear expansion coefficient α = 1.2 × 10⁻⁵ °C⁻¹) and the ring is 
made of aluminium (α = 2.3 × 10⁻⁵ °C⁻¹), predict what happens when both are heated equally and 
explain why.   
2. A flask is completely filled with water at 0 °C, fitted with a narrow glass tube, and heated slowly. 
The water level in the tube first falls slightly then rises. A separate Hope’s apparatus experiment 
shows water freezing only at the surface of a lake. 
a) Describe how the flask experiment demonstrates expansion of liquids.   
b) Using the anomalous expansion of water, explain with a sketch description why fish survive in a 
frozen lake in winter. At what temperature is water densest?   
3. A bicycle tyre contains 600 cm³ of air at a pressure of 1.0 atm (atmospheric pressure). The rider 
pumps more air, increasing the pressure to 2.5 atm while the temperature remains constant. 
Calculate the new volume of air inside the tyre using Boyle’s Law. Explain why the tyre feels harder.   
4. A weather balloon is filled with 4.0 L of helium at 27 °C on the ground. It rises to a height where the 
temperature drops to –73 °C. Pressure is assumed constant (balloon expands freely). 
Calculate the new volume of the balloon using Charles’s Law. Convert temperatures to Kelvin first.   
5. A sealed rigid metal can contains gas at a pressure of 1.5 atm and temperature of 27 °C. The can is 
placed in boiling water at 100 °C.  
Calculate the new pressure inside the can using Gay-Lussac’s Law.   
SAMPLE QUESTIONS ANSWERS 
Answers 1  
a) The metal ball expands in all directions when heated (linear expansion). Its diameter becomes 
larger than the inner diameter of the ring, so it no longer passes through. On cooling, it contracts and 
passes again. This shows solids expand on heating and contract on cooling.   
Answers2  
a) When heated, water expands, so the level in the narrow tube rises. This shows liquids expand on 
heating (real expansion greater than apparent because glass also expands slightly).

113 
 
b) Water contracts when cooled from 4 °C to 0 °C (anomalous behaviour). At 4 °C water is densest 
and sinks; colder water (0–4 °C) stays at the top and freezes. Ice (less dense) floats, insulating the 
water below at ~4 °C where fish can survive. Water is densest at 4 °C. 
Answers  3 
Boyle’s Law: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ (constant temperature)   
1.0 atm × 600 cm³ = 2.5 atm × V₂   
V₂ = (1.0 × 600) / 2.5 = 240 cm³   
The tyre feels harder because the same amount of air is now squeezed into a smaller volume, 
increasing pressure on the walls. 
Answers 4   
Convert: T₁ = 27 + 273 = 300 K; T₂ = –73 + 273 = 200 K   
Charles’s Law: V₁ / T₁ = V₂ / T₂   
V₂ = V₁ × (T₂ / T₁) = 4.0 × (200 / 300) = 2.67 L (approx.)   
The balloon shrinks because temperature decreased. 
Answers 5   
Convert: T₁ = 300 K; T₂ = 373 K   
Gay-Lussac’s Law: P₁ / T₁ = P₂ / T₂   
P₂ = P₁ × (T₂ / T₁) = 1.5 × (373 / 300) ≈ 1.865 atm   
The pressure increases, which is why aerosol cans warn “do not heat”.

SUB-TOPIC 2.2.4 INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINE (I.C.E) 
INTRODUCTION 
An internal combustion engine (I.C.E.) is a machine that converts chemical energy in fuel into 
mechanical energy. This happens when fuel burns inside the engine, producing heat and expanding 
gases that move parts of the engine to produce motion. 
 Specific Competence: Demonstrate how various internal combustion engine types operate 
(four stroke engine). 
   Key terms 
 Spark Ignition (SI) Engine: An internal combustion engine where the air–fuel mixture is 
ignited by a spark plug to produce power.  
 Compression Ignition (CI) Engine: Air is compressed to a very high pressure and 
temperature, causing it to ignite automatically without a spark plug.  
 A rotary engine: Is an internal combustion engine that uses a rotating triangular motor instead 
of pistons to convert fuel energy into mechanical motion. 
 Hybrid Engine: A hybrid engine is a system that combines a conventional internal combustion 
engine (ICE) with an electric motor and battery. 
  Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition (HCCI): A premixed air–fuel mixture (like in 
petrol engines) is compressed until it auto-ignites (like in diesel engines). 
 Fuel Cell Engine: A fuel cell engine generates electricity through a chemical reaction between 
hydrogen and oxygen instead of burning fuel. 
Learning Activities 
These learning activities will help learners to demonstrate how various internal combustion engines 
operate (four stroke engine).

cylinders to produce power.   
(b) It converts chemical energy in fuel into mechanical energy to move vehicles. Most buses, trucks, 
cars, and mining vehicles in Zambia use ICEs. Without them, public transport, delivery of goods, and 
mining operations would stop. 
Answer 2   
It is called an internal combustion engine because the fuel burns (combusts) **inside** the engine 
cylinders, not outside like in a steam engine. 
Answer 3  
Petrol (for spark-ignition engines) and diesel (for compression-ignition engines). 
Answer 4  
1. Intake stroke: Piston moves down, intake valve opens → air-fuel mixture enters the cylinder.   
2. Compression stroke: Piston moves up, both valves closed → mixture is compressed.   
3. Power (ignition) stroke: Spark plug produces a spark → mixture explodes, pushing piston down 
(this turns the crankshaft).   
4. Exhaust stroke: Piston moves up, exhaust valve opens → waste gases leave the cylinder.   
The cycle then repeats. This is how the engine produces continuous power.

125 
 
Answer 5   
(a) Air is compressed so much that its temperature rises very high. Diesel fuel is injected at the top of 
the compression stroke and ignites automatically (no spark plug).   
(b) Diesel engines produce more torque (pulling power), which is needed for heavy loads in mines. 
Answer 6   
Four-stroke engines are more efficient, produce less pollution, and last longer than two-stroke 
engines. They complete the cycle in four separate strokes, allowing better control of fuel and air. 
Answer 7   
- Cylinder and piston   
- Crankshaft and connecting rod   
- Intake and exhaust valves   
- Spark plug (petrol) or fuel injector (diesel)   
- Camshaft   
- Flywheel

INTRODUCTION 
Heat Transfer covers how heat moves through conduction, convection, and radiation, with 
applications in daily life and technology. 
Specific competence  
 Apply the concepts of heat transfer. 
Key terms  
 Conduction: Process of heat transfer through a medium (material e.g solids, liquids, and 
gases). 
 Radiation: Process of heat transfer as electromagnetic waves through space (e.g heat from 
the stove, sun, ...). 
 Convection: Process of heat transfer due to the movement of fluid (e.g water, cooking oil, 
air,.)

learning through hands-on activities and problem-solving tasks.

 Key Learning Points             
  Heat is transferred from hotter to colder regions in three main ways:   
 Conduction: Heat travels through solids as vibrating particles pass energy to neighbouring 
particles (no overall movement of matter). Metals (e.g. copper) are good conductors; wood, 
plastic and air are poor conductors (insulators).   
  Convection: Heat travels in fluids (liquids and gases) by the movement of the fluid itself. 
Hotter, less dense fluid rises and cooler fluid sinks, forming convection currents (e.g. sea/land 
breezes, refrigerators).   
 Radiation: Heat travels as electromagnetic waves (infrared) through empty space. No 
medium is needed.

radiant energy.   
3. In a village near Kitwe, people often cook nshima in large pots over open fires. Hot air rises from 
the fire, while cooler air sinks, helping to circulate heat around the cooking area.   
   (a) Name the process responsible for this heat circulation.   
   (b) Draw a simple labelled diagram to show the convection currents in the air above the fire.

139 
 
and place it in a cardboard box lined with old newspapers and cotton cloth.   
    (a) Identify which heat transfer process(es) each material helps to reduce and explain how.   
    (b) Suggest one improvement they could make using recycled materials to further reduce heat

5. In a refrigerator, cold air near the cooling coils sinks, while warmer air rises, creating a continuous

    Explain how convection helps maintain a uniform low temperature inside the refrigerator. 
SAMPLE QUESTION ANSWERS 
1. (a) The metal spoon is a good conductor of heat. Its particles vibrate vigorously and quickly pass 
kinetic energy to the hand. The plastic spoon is a poor conductor (insulator), so energy transfer is 
very slow.   
   (b) Wooden or plastic handles are poor conductors; they prevent heat from reaching the hand and 
avoid burns.   
2. (a) Radiation (infrared rays from the sun).   
   (b) Dark (dull/black) surfaces are good absorbers of radiant heat and therefore become hotter. 
Shiny surfaces are poor absorbers (good reflectors) of radiation, so they stay cooler. Good absorbers 
are also good emitters.   
3. (a) Convection.   
   (b) Diagram: arrows showing hot air rising from the fire, spreading out, cooling and sinking back 
down to the fire in a continuous loop (convection current).   
4. (a) Aluminium foil (shiny) – reduces radiation (reflects heat).   
     Newspapers and cotton cloth (poor conductors) – reduce conduction.   
     Cardboard box – reduces convection by limiting air movement.   
   (b) Add a tight-fitting lid (stops convection at the top) or extra layers of polystyrene foam/old cloth 
for better insulation.   
5. Convection currents circulate air inside the refrigerator: cold air near the cooling coils sinks (being 
denser), warmer air rises, and the continuous flow keeps the temperature even throughout the fridge.

SUB-TOPIC 2.2.6: MEASUREMENT OF HEAT 
INTRODUCTION  
Measurement of heat focuses on quantifying heat using concepts like heat capacity and latent heat, 
emphasizing practical experiments and numerical problem-solving. This subtopic builds on kinetic 
theory from earlier sections and prepare learners for advanced topics like renewable energy in Form 
4. 
Specific competence  
 Solve practical and numerical problems involving measurement of heat. 
Key terms 
 Heat Capacity: Is the amount of heat energy required to change the temperature of a 
substance by one degree Celsius (1OC) or one Kelvin (1K)  
 Specific Heat Capacity: Is the amount of heat energy required to change the temperature of 
1 kg of a substance by 1OC or 1K.  
 Latent Heat: Is the amount of heat energy required to change the state (solid, liquid, and 
gas) of a substance without change in temperature. 
 Specific Latent Heat of Fusion: Is the amount of energy required to change 1 kg of a 
substance from solid to liquid without changing its temperature.  
 Specific Latent heat of vaporization: Is the amount of energy required to change 1 kg of a 
substance from liquid to gas at its boiling point without changing its temperature. 
 
Learning Activities 
This sub-topic, has 5 learning activities as outlined in the syllabus focusing on hands-on-activities 
and problem-solving tasks.

SAMPLE QUESTIONS 
1. A learner heats 0.5 kg of cooking oil (specific heat capacity ≈ 2000 J kg⁻¹ °C⁻¹) and 0.5 kg of 
water (specific heat capacity = 4180 J kg⁻¹ °C⁻¹) using the same heat source for the same amount of 
time. Both start at 25 °C.   
   (a) Which liquid will have a higher temperature after heating? Explain your answer using the 
concept of specific heat capacity.   
   (b) Relate this to why water is often used in cooling systems (e.g., car radiators) instead of oil.   
2. During cooking, a pot of water is brought to the boil at 100 °C. Even though the stove continues 
to supply heat, the temperature of the water stays at 100 °C while it boils away.   
   (a) Name the type of heat involved here and explain why the temperature does not rise.   
   (b) Calculate the heat energy required to completely vaporize 0.2 kg of boiling water at 100 °C 
(specific latent heat of vaporization of water = 2 260 000 J kg⁻¹). Show your working.   
3. A learner adds 0.1 kg of ice at 0 °C to 0.4 kg of warm water at 40 °C in an insulated cup. After 
the ice melts completely, the final temperature is 28 °C.   
   (a) Explain why heat is absorbed even though the temperature of the ice stays at 0 °C during 
melting.   
   (b) Name the two processes involved in the heat transfer to the ice (one for melting and one for 
warming the resulting water).   
4. In a Zambian household, people often place hot charcoal in a brazier to warm a room during cold 
evenings. The room heats up gradually, but the metal brazier becomes very hot quickly.   
    (a) Identify the main heat transfer process that heats the brazier itself and the process that warms 
the air in the room.   
    (b) If the brazier has a mass of 2 kg and is made of iron (specific heat capacity ≈ 450 J kg⁻¹ °C⁻¹), 
calculate the heat absorbed by the brazier when its temperature rises from 25 °C to 200 °C.   
5. A solar water heater panel painted black is placed in direct sunlight. It absorbs heat quickly and 
transfers it to water flowing through pipes.   
    (a) Explain why black paint is used instead of shiny silver paint.   
    (b) Discuss how understanding specific heat capacity and latent heat could help improve the 
design to store heat for use at night. 
SAMPLE QUESTIONS ANSWERS 
1. (a) Cooking oil will have the higher temperature. It has a lower specific heat capacity, so the 
same amount of heat produces a larger temperature rise (ΔT = Q / (m c)).

151 
 
   (b) Water’s high specific heat capacity means it can absorb a lot of heat with only a small 
temperature increase – ideal for carrying away heat in car radiators without boiling quickly.   
2. (a) Latent heat of vaporization. The heat energy is used to overcome forces between particles and 
change liquid water into vapour (change of state), not to increase the kinetic energy of the particles, 
so temperature remains constant at 100 °C.   
   (b) Q = m Lᵥ   
      Q = 0.2 kg × 2 260 000 J kg⁻¹ = 452 000 J   
3. (a) The heat is used as latent heat of fusion to break the bonds between ice particles and change it 
into liquid water without raising the temperature.   
   (b) (i) Latent heat of fusion (melting the ice at 0 °C)   
        (ii) Specific heat capacity of water (warming the melted water from 0 °C to 28 °C).   
4. (a) Conduction heats the metal brazier quickly. Convection then carries the heat from the hot 
brazier to warm the air in the room.   
    (b) Q = m c ΔT   
       ΔT = 200 °C – 25 °C = 175 °C   
       Q = 2 kg × 450 J kg⁻¹ °C⁻¹ × 175 °C = 157 500 J   
5. (a) Black paint is a good absorber of radiant heat (infrared) from the sun, so the panel heats up 
faster. Shiny silver paint reflects most radiation and absorbs very little.   
    (b) Use materials with high specific heat capacity (or phase-change materials with high latent 
heat) inside the panel or in a storage tank. These can absorb large amounts of heat during the day 
and release it slowly at night without large temperature drops.
